questions.question: Honey found in Egyptian tombs over 3,000 years old was still perfectly edible when discovered. What three properties of honey make it essentially impossible for bacteria to survive in it?
questions.correct_answer: Low moisture, high acidity, and natural hydrogen peroxide production
alternative_answers: it's too acidic and dry for bacteria
questions.source_excerpt: Honey's extremely low moisture content (~17%), acidic pH (~3.9), and enzymatic production of small amounts of hydrogen peroxide create an environment where bacteria cannot grow, giving honey an essentially indefinite shelf life.

reasoning.why_interesting: Everyone knows honey lasts a long time, but the triple-mechanism explanation reveals elegant chemistry behind a familiar food. The 3,000-year Egyptian tomb detail anchors the surprise.
reasoning.universal_appeal: Honey is consumed and recognized worldwide. No cultural specificity.
reasoning.boring_check: Not a 'What is' format. The narrative setup (Egyptian tombs) adds story. The 'three properties' framing invites reasoning. Passes all checks.

self_critique.reasoning: Strong educational question with multiple reasoning paths. Players can guess acidity or dryness. The hydrogen peroxide component is the true surprise. Accepts partial answers generously.

questions.question: Lake Baikal in Siberia holds roughly 20% of the world's unfrozen surface fresh water — more than all five North American Great Lakes combined. Is it that large because of its surface area, its extraordinary depth, or both?
questions.correct_answer: Its extraordinary depth — it is the deepest lake on Earth at over 1,600 metres
alternative_answers: because it is the deepest lake in the world
questions.source_excerpt: Lake Baikal is the world's deepest lake at 1,642 metres. Despite being only the 7th largest by surface area, its extreme depth gives it a volume of 23,615 km³ — about 20% of Earth's unfrozen surface fresh water.

reasoning.why_interesting: The idea that one lake holds more fresh water than all five Great Lakes combined is staggering. The twist — it's depth, not surface area — adds a layer of surprise.
reasoning.universal_appeal: Fresh water, lakes, and the Great Lakes comparison are globally understandable. Lake Baikal is internationally famous.
reasoning.boring_check: Not a simple 'what is the deepest lake' format. The comparison to the Great Lakes and the three-option framing make it engaging. Passes all checks.

self_critique.reasoning: Strong scale surprise with a reasoning path. Players can think: 'the Great Lakes are huge, so this must be massive — but it's not the biggest lake by area, so it must be incredibly deep.' Well-structured three-way choice.

questions.question: Venus rotates so slowly that a single day on Venus (one full rotation) takes longer than a Venusian year (one orbit around the Sun). Roughly how many Earth days does one Venus day last: 10, 100, or 240?
questions.source_excerpt: Venus takes approximately 243 Earth days to complete one rotation on its axis, while its orbital period is only about 225 Earth days, meaning a day on Venus is longer than its year.

reasoning.why_interesting: The notion that a day can be longer than a year is deeply counterintuitive. It violates our basic temporal assumptions. The estimation format lets players reason about scale.
reasoning.universal_appeal: Venus and basic planetary concepts are taught worldwide. Universally accessible.
reasoning.boring_check: Not a boring format. The paradox (day > year) is the hook. Three estimation options span a wide range. Passes all checks.

self_critique.reasoning: The day-longer-than-year paradox is one of astronomy's most delightful facts. The three options allow estimation. High engagement path — players reason from the paradox setup.

questions.question: China stretches nearly 5,200 km from east to west — wide enough to span five geographical time zones. Yet the entire country operates on a single official time zone. What does this mean for people in China's far west — does their sunrise come unusually early, or unusually late?
questions.correct_answer: Unusually late — the sun may not rise until after 10 AM in winter in western China
questions.source_excerpt: China spans five geographical time zones (approximately 73°E to 135°E) but officially uses only one: China Standard Time (UTC+8), set to Beijing time. In Kashgar, Xinjiang, sunrise can occur as late as 10:15 AM in winter.

reasoning.why_interesting: The idea of sunrise at 10 AM is deeply strange and vivid. It reveals how political decisions override natural geography, with real consequences for daily life.
reasoning.universal_appeal: Time zones, sunrise, and China are universally known concepts. No niche knowledge required.
reasoning.boring_check: Not 'What is the capital' format. The binary choice (early or late) invites reasoning — players must think about which direction the offset goes. Passes all checks.

self_critique.reasoning: Strong question with a vivid real-world consequence. Players can reason: Beijing is in the east, so the single time zone is set to eastern China, meaning western China's clock runs 'ahead' of the sun — sunrise comes late. The 10 AM sunrise detail is memorable.

questions.question: The driest place on Earth isn't in any desert you might expect. Parts of it haven't seen rain or snow for an estimated 2 million years. Is it in: the Sahara, the Atacama Desert, or Antarctica?
questions.source_excerpt: The McMurdo Dry Valleys in Antarctica are the driest place on Earth. Katabatic winds evaporate all moisture, and some areas have received no precipitation for an estimated 2 million years. Antarctica itself is technically the world's largest desert.

reasoning.why_interesting: Most people associate 'driest place' with hot sandy deserts. Antarctica being drier than the Sahara upends expectations completely.
reasoning.universal_appeal: All three locations are globally famous. The concept of 'driest place' is universally understandable.
reasoning.boring_check: Three-option format with a surprising correct answer. Not 'What is the driest place' — the framing hints at an unexpected answer. Passes all checks.

self_critique.reasoning: Classic misconception buster. The 2-million-year figure is staggering. Players can reason: 'The Sahara gets some rain, the Atacama is very dry, but Antarctica... it's all ice, not rain.' The logic path is engaging.

questions.question: Glass appears to be a solid, but physicists have long debated its true nature. At the molecular level, glass lacks the ordered crystal structure of a true solid. Is glass technically classified as a solid, a liquid, or something else entirely?
questions.correct_answer: An amorphous solid — it has the rigidity of a solid but the disordered molecular structure of a liquid
questions.source_excerpt: Glass is classified as an amorphous solid — it has the mechanical rigidity of a solid but lacks the long-range ordered crystal lattice structure. It is not a 'supercooled liquid' as the popular myth suggests, but a distinct state of matter.

reasoning.why_interesting: The popular myth that 'glass is a liquid' has circulated for decades, often supported by the old claim about medieval windows being thicker at the bottom. The true answer — amorphous solid — is more nuanced and interesting.
reasoning.universal_appeal: Glass is an everyday material worldwide. The solid-vs-liquid question is universally intriguing.
reasoning.boring_check: Not a boring format. The three-way choice (solid, liquid, something else) engages reasoning. The 'something else' answer is satisfying. Passes all checks.

self_critique.reasoning: Good misconception buster that challenges the common 'glass is a liquid' myth. The three-way framing invites genuine thought. Educational value is high — introduces the concept of amorphous solids.

questions.question: Africa is often shown on maps as roughly the same size as Greenland. In reality, how many Greenlands could fit inside Africa: 3, 7, or 14?
questions.source_excerpt: Africa has a land area of approximately 30.37 million km², while Greenland covers about 2.17 million km². This means Africa is roughly 14 times larger than Greenland, despite Mercator projection maps making them appear similar in size.

reasoning.why_interesting: The Mercator projection has distorted our perception of Africa's size for centuries. Discovering Africa is 14x larger than Greenland — not roughly equal — is a powerful visual correction.
reasoning.universal_appeal: Maps, Africa, and Greenland are universally known. The Mercator distortion affects everyone's spatial understanding.
reasoning.boring_check: Three options spanning a wide range (3, 7, 14). The most extreme answer is correct, which is the ideal setup. Not a boring format. Passes all checks.

self_critique.reasoning: Exceptional scale surprise. Nearly everyone has internalized the Mercator distortion. The estimation format (3, 7, or 14) makes it engaging and the correct answer is the most extreme — maximum surprise. Clear, unambiguous, and globally relevant.

questions.question: There are 206 bones in the adult human body. At birth, roughly how many bones does a baby have: fewer than an adult, the same number, or more?
questions.correct_answer: More — about 270 bones, which gradually fuse together as the child grows
questions.source_excerpt: A newborn baby has approximately 270 bones, many of which are made of cartilage. As the child grows, many of these bones fuse together, resulting in the 206 bones found in a typical adult skeleton.

reasoning.why_interesting: Most people assume babies have fewer bones since they're smaller. The reverse is true — they have about 64 more — which creates a genuine surprise.
reasoning.universal_appeal: Human biology is universally relevant. No cultural specificity.
reasoning.boring_check: Three-option choice format avoids boring 'how many' recall. The counterintuitive answer (more, not fewer) is the hook. Passes all checks.

self_critique.reasoning: Clean counterintuitive truth. The three-way choice (fewer, same, more) is perfectly structured — the intuitive answer is wrong. The bone-fusion explanation is satisfying and educational.

questions.question: Only two countries in the world are doubly landlocked — meaning they are surrounded entirely by other landlocked countries, so you must cross at least two borders to reach an ocean. One is Uzbekistan. Name the other.
questions.source_excerpt: Only two countries are doubly landlocked: Uzbekistan (surrounded by Afghanistan, Kazakhstan, Kyrgyzstan, Tajikistan, and Turkmenistan — all landlocked) and Liechtenstein (surrounded by Austria and Switzerland — both landlocked).

reasoning.why_interesting: The concept of 'doubly landlocked' is itself a delightful geographical idea that most people have never considered. The pairing of tiny Liechtenstein with vast Uzbekistan is charming.
reasoning.universal_appeal: Countries and oceans are universally understood. The concept is accessible once explained.
reasoning.boring_check: Not a boring format. The concept of 'doubly landlocked' is the hook — it's a geographical property most have never thought about. Giving one answer (Uzbekistan) helps players reason toward the other. Passes all checks.

self_critique.reasoning: The concept itself teaches something new. Players can reason: 'Which tiny European countries are surrounded only by other landlocked countries? Switzerland is landlocked, Austria is landlocked...' The engagement path exists.

questions.question: Octopuses have blue blood, three hearts, and a doughnut-shaped brain. But perhaps most remarkably, about two-thirds of their neurons are not in their brain at all. Where are the majority of an octopus's neurons located?
questions.source_excerpt: An octopus has approximately 500 million neurons, with about two-thirds (roughly 350 million) located in its arms rather than its central brain. Each arm can taste, touch, and make decisions somewhat independently.

reasoning.why_interesting: The idea that an octopus's arms can essentially 'think' independently challenges our brain-centric view of intelligence. The setup listing other oddities (blue blood, three hearts, doughnut brain) builds anticipation.
reasoning.universal_appeal: Octopuses are globally fascinating. Neuroscience concepts are universally accessible when framed this way.
reasoning.boring_check: The narrative setup builds intrigue. The 'where are the neurons' question invites reasoning — players know octopus arms are dexterous and might guess correctly. Passes all checks.

self_critique.reasoning: Excellent biological oddity. The layered setup (blue blood, three hearts, doughnut brain) primes the player for something even more surprising. Players who know octopuses are remarkably dexterous can reason toward 'arms.' Highly memorable.

questions.question: Istanbul is the only major city in the world that sits on two continents. But which two continents does it straddle?
questions.source_excerpt: Istanbul straddles the Bosphorus strait, with its western portion in Europe and eastern portion in Asia. It is the world's only major city sitting on two continents, with a metropolitan population of over 15 million.

reasoning.why_interesting: The fact that a single city sits on two continents is a vivid geographical idea. It makes you think about borders in a physical way.
reasoning.universal_appeal: Istanbul and the concept of continents are universally known.
reasoning.boring_check: This is well-known and somewhat straightforward. While not boring in format, the surprise factor is modest for knowledgeable players.

self_critique.reasoning: Solid easy question. Very clear and universally accessible. The surprise is moderate — many people know this — but it works well at easy difficulty where the answer should be gettable.

questions.question: Every second, the Sun converts roughly 4 million tonnes of its own mass into energy via nuclear fusion. At this rate, has the Sun lost a noticeable fraction of its mass since it formed 4.6 billion years ago? Closer to 0.01%, 0.1%, or 5%?
questions.correct_answer: About 0.03% — a tiny fraction despite the enormous rate
questions.source_excerpt: The Sun converts about 4.26 million tonnes of mass to energy per second. Over 4.6 billion years, this totals roughly 6×10^26 kg — about 0.03% of its total mass of 2×10^30 kg. Despite the staggering rate, the Sun is so massive that the loss is negligible.

reasoning.why_interesting: 4 million tonnes per SECOND sounds catastrophic, but the Sun is so inconceivably massive that 4.6 billion years of this barely dents it. The juxtaposition between the terrifying rate and the negligible percentage is the surprise.
reasoning.universal_appeal: The Sun and basic physics concepts are universally accessible.
reasoning.boring_check: Three estimation options spanning orders of magnitude. The setup primes you to think the loss is huge, but the answer is tiny. Excellent misdirection. Passes all checks.

self_critique.reasoning: Perfect estimation challenge. The setup (4 million tonnes per second for billions of years) leads most players to overestimate. The tiny answer reveals just how massive the Sun truly is. High engagement — players can attempt the mental math.

questions.question: The Pacific Ocean is so vast that it contains a point called Point Nemo — the spot on Earth farthest from any land. The nearest humans to Point Nemo are often not on any ship or island. Where are they?
questions.correct_answer: Astronauts on the International Space Station, orbiting about 400 km overhead
questions.source_excerpt: Point Nemo (48°52.6'S 123°23.6'W) is the oceanic pole of inaccessibility, about 2,688 km from the nearest land. The ISS orbits at roughly 408 km altitude, so when it passes overhead, astronauts are the closest humans — far nearer than anyone on land.

reasoning.why_interesting: The idea that the nearest humans to a point on Earth's surface are in outer space is a profound and delightful connection between geography and space exploration. It reframes our sense of remoteness.
reasoning.universal_appeal: The ocean, remoteness, and the ISS are universally understood concepts.
reasoning.boring_check: The narrative setup is compelling. The 'where are they?' question invites creative reasoning. The answer connects geography to space — unexpected and memorable. Passes all checks.

self_critique.reasoning: One of the most delightful geographical facts available. The surprise is genuine and the reasoning path exists — players might think 'submarines? planes?' before the stunning answer. The connection between deep ocean remoteness and space is poetic.

questions.question: Bananas are slightly radioactive due to their potassium content. Scientists even created an informal unit of radiation measurement named after them. How many bananas would you need to eat in a single sitting to receive a dangerous radiation dose: about 100, about 10,000, or more than 10 million?
questions.source_excerpt: The 'banana equivalent dose' (BED) is an informal radiation unit. One banana delivers about 0.1 microsieverts. A lethal acute dose is roughly 4-5 sieverts, requiring approximately 40-50 million bananas consumed simultaneously — the body also excretes excess potassium, making real danger practically impossible.

reasoning.why_interesting: The 'radioactive banana' is a famous science fun fact, but quantifying how many you'd need to eat makes it concrete and surprising. The absurd scale (tens of millions) puts radiation fears in perspective.
reasoning.universal_appeal: Bananas are eaten worldwide. Radiation is a universally understood concept.
reasoning.boring_check: Three options spanning five orders of magnitude. The most extreme answer is correct. Not a boring format — the banana/radiation connection is inherently amusing. Passes all checks.

self_critique.reasoning: Great estimation challenge that makes radiation tangible and non-scary. The 'banana equivalent dose' is a real concept used by nuclear physicists. Players can reason: 'bananas seem harmless, so it must be a lot' — the answer validates that intuition spectacularly.

questions.question: There is a country in Africa where over 800 languages are spoken — more than any other country on Earth. Is it Nigeria, Democratic Republic of Congo, or Papua New Guinea?
questions.correct_answer: Papua New Guinea — which is actually not in Africa but in Oceania, with over 840 languages
alternative_answers: trick question - Papua New Guinea is not in Africa
questions.source_excerpt: Papua New Guinea has over 840 living languages — the most linguistically diverse country on Earth. It is located in Oceania, not Africa. Nigeria ranks second with over 520 languages.

reasoning.why_interesting: The question sets up the assumption that the answer must be African, then reveals the trick — PNG isn't in Africa at all, but is the actual answer. Double surprise: the country isn't where you expect, and Africa doesn't hold the record.
reasoning.universal_appeal: Languages, countries, and continents are universally understood.
reasoning.boring_check: The misdirection is the clever framing here — the question says 'a country in Africa' then includes PNG as an option. Attentive listeners catch the trick. Passes all checks.

self_critique.reasoning: Clever misdirection question that works on multiple levels. Players who catch that PNG isn't in Africa get a satisfying 'gotcha' moment. Those who don't still learn about linguistic diversity. Minor clarity concern: some players might feel tricked, but that's the lateral thinking element.
